channel.title: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis (no repeats)
channel.description: Table of Contents for Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis. List of articles from both the latest and EarlyView issues.
channel.dc:title: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis (no repeats)

image.title: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Simple and Mild Synthesis of Bis‐CF3‐Cyclobutane Derivatives via Photoinduced Regioselective Dimerization of 1,3‐Enynes
item.dc:description:
Photo‐induced chemoselective and regioselective head‐to‐head dimerization of β‐CF3‐1,3‐enyne to synthesis of bis‐CF3‐cyclobutanes with the alkynyl group intact was developed. No head‐to‐tail dimerization isomers were observed. This protocol operates under mild conditions, requires only a photocatalyst and a base, and exhibits broad functional group tolerance.
Cyclobutane derivatives are versatile structural motifs in natural products, pharmaceuticals. Cyclobutanes obtained from 1,3‐enynes are rare and suffer from poor yield, low chemoselectivity, and regioselectivity. Herein, we disclose a simple and mild synthesis of bis‐CF3‐cyclobutanes via photoinduced chemoselective and regioselective head‐to‐head dimerization of β‐CF3‐1,3‐enyne with the alkynyl group intact. No head‐to‐tail dimerization isomers were observed. This reaction features simple and mild conditions, readily available substrates, good regioselectivity, broad substrate scope, and high functional group tolerance. This reaction was applied successfully to the late‐stage modification of drugs and natural products.
item.content:encoded:
Photo-induced chemoselective and regioselective head-to-head dimerization of β-CF3-1,3-enyne to synthesis of bis-CF3-cyclobutanes with the alkynyl group intact was developed. No head-to-tail dimerization isomers were observed. This protocol operates under mild conditions, requires only a photocatalyst and a base, and exhibits broad functional group tolerance.


Cyclobutane derivatives are versatile structural motifs in natural products, pharmaceuticals. Cyclobutanes obtained from 1,3-enynes are rare and suffer from poor yield, low chemoselectivity, and regioselectivity. Herein, we disclose a simple and mild synthesis of bis-CF3-cyclobutanes via photoinduced chemoselective and regioselective head-to-head dimerization of β-CF3-1,3-enyne with the alkynyl group intact. No head-to-tail dimerization isomers were observed. This reaction features simple and mild conditions, readily available substrates, good regioselectivity, broad substrate scope, and high functional group tolerance. This reaction was applied successfully to the late-stage modification of drugs and natural products.
item.dc:creator: Pei‐Yan Ma, Li‐Juan Tang, Yongshun Wen, Yun Liao, Yingtong Zong, Zhengwang Chen, Zhen‐Hua Zhang, Lieqiang Liao, Daohong Yu
item.dc:title: Simple and Mild Synthesis of Bis‐CF3‐Cyclobutane Derivatives via Photoinduced Regioselective Dimerization of 1,3‐Enynes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: CH Halogenation via Directing Group Strategies With Earth‐Abundant Metals (Cu, Co, Ni, Fe): Scope, Mechanism, and Limitations
item.dc:description:
Halogenated hydrocarbons, encompassing aliphatic, alicyclic, aromatic, and heterocyclic frameworks, are indispensable structural motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, advanced materials, and natural product synthesis. The direct functionalization of inert CH bonds through transition‐metal‐catalyzed halogenation has emerged as a powerful, step‐economical, and atom‐efficient strategy for the construction. In pursuit of sustainable catalysis, significant research efforts have recently focused on employing earth‐abundant, cost‐effective, and environmentally benign first‐row transition metals, notably copper, cobalt, nickel, and iron, as catalysts for these transformations. This review provides a systematic and comprehensive overview of recent advances in the halogenation of (hetero)aromatic and inert aliphatic CH bonds catalyzed by such economical metals. The content is organized according to the catalytic metal center, with each section discussing substrate scope, regioselectivity control, practical reaction conditions, and proposed mechanistic pathways. Key comparisons are drawn regarding the reactivity, selectivity, and functional group tolerance of different catalytic systems. Finally, the review outlines current methodological limitations and highlights promising future directions aimed at enhancing catalytic efficiency, broadening substrate generality, improving selectivity prediction, and developing truly sustainable and scalable halogenation protocols. The insights presented aim to guide the rational design of next‐generation CH functionalization methodologies.
Halogenated hydrocarbons, encompassing aliphatic, alicyclic, aromatic, and heterocyclic frameworks, are indispensable structural motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, advanced materials, and natural product synthesis. The direct functionalization of inert CH bonds through transition‐metal‐catalyzed halogenation has emerged as a powerful, step‐economical, and atom‐efficient strategy for the construction. In pursuit of sustainable catalysis, significant research efforts have recently focused on employing earth‐abundant, cost‐effective, and environmentally benign first‐row transition metals, notably copper, cobalt, nickel, and iron, as catalysts for these transformations. This review provides a systematic and comprehensive overview of recent advances in the halogenation of (hetero)aromatic and inert aliphatic CH bonds catalyzed by such economical metals. The content is organized according to the catalytic metal center, with each section discussing substrate scope, regioselectivity control, practical reaction conditions, and proposed mechanistic pathways. Key comparisons are drawn regarding the reactivity, selectivity, and functional group tolerance of different catalytic systems. Finally, the review outlines current methodological limitations and highlights promising future directions aimed at enhancing catalytic efficiency, broadening substrate generality, improving selectivity prediction, and developing truly sustainable and scalable halogenation protocols. The insights presented aim to guide the rational design of next‐generation CH functionalization methodologies.
item.content:encoded:
Halogenated hydrocarbons, encompassing aliphatic, alicyclic, aromatic, and heterocyclic frameworks, are indispensable structural motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, advanced materials, and natural product synthesis. The direct functionalization of inert CH bonds through transition-metal-catalyzed halogenation has emerged as a powerful, step-economical, and atom-efficient strategy for the construction. In pursuit of sustainable catalysis, significant research efforts have recently focused on employing earth-abundant, cost-effective, and environmentally benign first-row transition metals, notably copper, cobalt, nickel, and iron, as catalysts for these transformations. This review provides a systematic and comprehensive overview of recent advances in the halogenation of (hetero)aromatic and inert aliphatic CH bonds catalyzed by such economical metals. The content is organized according to the catalytic metal center, with each section discussing substrate scope, regioselectivity control, practical reaction conditions, and proposed mechanistic pathways. Key comparisons are drawn regarding the reactivity, selectivity, and functional group tolerance of different catalytic systems. Finally, the review outlines current methodological limitations and highlights promising future directions aimed at enhancing catalytic efficiency, broadening substrate generality, improving selectivity prediction, and developing truly sustainable and scalable halogenation protocols. The insights presented aim to guide the rational design of next-generation CH functionalization methodologies.


Halogenated hydrocarbons, encompassing aliphatic, alicyclic, aromatic, and heterocyclic frameworks, are indispensable structural motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, advanced materials, and natural product synthesis. The direct functionalization of inert CH bonds through transition-metal-catalyzed halogenation has emerged as a powerful, step-economical, and atom-efficient strategy for the construction. In pursuit of sustainable catalysis, significant research efforts have recently focused on employing earth-abundant, cost-effective, and environmentally benign first-row transition metals, notably copper, cobalt, nickel, and iron, as catalysts for these transformations. This review provides a systematic and comprehensive overview of recent advances in the halogenation of (hetero)aromatic and inert aliphatic CH bonds catalyzed by such economical metals. The content is organized according to the catalytic metal center, with each section discussing substrate scope, regioselectivity control, practical reaction conditions, and proposed mechanistic pathways. Key comparisons are drawn regarding the reactivity, selectivity, and functional group tolerance of different catalytic systems. Finally, the review outlines current methodological limitations and highlights promising future directions aimed at enhancing catalytic efficiency, broadening substrate generality, improving selectivity prediction, and developing truly sustainable and scalable halogenation protocols. The insights presented aim to guide the rational design of next-generation CH functionalization methodologies.
item.dc:creator: Huicheng Cheng, Jiaoli Ma
item.dc:title: CH Halogenation via Directing Group Strategies With Earth‐Abundant Metals (Cu, Co, Ni, Fe): Scope, Mechanism, and Limitations

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: N‐Fluoroalkylthiolation of Amines and Sulfonamides with Fluoroalkyl Sulfoxide as Fluoroalkylthiolating Reagent
item.dc:description:
The B(C6F5)3‐promoted N‐fluoroalkylthiolation reaction of amines with fluoroalkanesulfenic acids formed in situ from fluoroalkyl sulfoxides was achieved, affording a series of fluoroalkanesulfenamides. In the presence of Tf2O, the reaction of sulfonamides with fluoroalkyl sulfoxides occurred readily to give the corresponding N‐fluoroalkylthiolation products.
B(C6F5)3‐promoted N‐fluoroalkylthiolation reaction of amines with fluoroalkanesulfenic acids formed in situ from fluoroalkyl sulfoxides was successfully achieved, affording a series of fluoroalkanesulfenamides, while no N‐fluoroalkylthiolation product was formed with sulfonamides under similar conditions. Notably, in the presence of Tf2O, the reaction of sulfonamides with fluoroalkyl sulfoxides proceeded smoothly at room temperature to give the corresponding N‐fluoroalkylthiolation products. Two possible reaction pathways were proposed based on experimental results.
item.content:encoded:
The B(C6F5)3-promoted N-fluoroalkylthiolation reaction of amines with fluoroalkanesulfenic acids formed in situ from fluoroalkyl sulfoxides was achieved, affording a series of fluoroalkanesulfenamides. In the presence of Tf2O, the reaction of sulfonamides with fluoroalkyl sulfoxides occurred readily to give the corresponding N-fluoroalkylthiolation products.


B(C6F5)3-promoted N-fluoroalkylthiolation reaction of amines with fluoroalkanesulfenic acids formed in situ from fluoroalkyl sulfoxides was successfully achieved, affording a series of fluoroalkanesulfenamides, while no N-fluoroalkylthiolation product was formed with sulfonamides under similar conditions. Notably, in the presence of Tf2O, the reaction of sulfonamides with fluoroalkyl sulfoxides proceeded smoothly at room temperature to give the corresponding N-fluoroalkylthiolation products. Two possible reaction pathways were proposed based on experimental results.
item.dc:creator: Chen Du, Min Jiang, Jin‐Tao Liu
item.dc:title: N‐Fluoroalkylthiolation of Amines and Sulfonamides with Fluoroalkyl Sulfoxide as Fluoroalkylthiolating Reagent

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Aldol Reactions of Germyl Dienolates With Precise Control Over Regioselectivity and Perfect Diastereoselectivity
item.dc:description:
Achieving selective aldol reactions using dienolates as nucleophiles is challenging due to the potential formation of up to eight different regioisomeric and stereoisomeric aldol adducts. This study demonstrates that Ge dienolates can facilitate highly diastereoselective aldol reactions. Furthermore, the key role of the solvents is also clarified. The solvent‐dependent reversibility of the α‐addition provides precise control over the regioselectivity. Both regioselectivity and diastereoselectivity are influenced by the choice of solvent and the presence of Lewis acids.
Enolates are essential nucleophiles for the formation of CC bonds. In particular, dienolates, i.e., π‐extended enolates that contain a conjugated additional CC bond alongside the enolate moiety, are valuable nucleophiles for the synthesis of π‐conjugated hydroxy ketones. Alas, selective aldol reactions of dienolates remain challenging because they can yield up to eight possible regioisomeric (α‐ or γ‐position) and stereoisomeric (syn or anti) aldol adducts. Here, we present the synthesis of Ge dienolates via the (4 + 1) cycloaddition of allenones and GeCl2 together with an investigation of their reactivity. Facilitated by the rigidity of their cyclic structures, the Ge dienolates undergo highly diastereoselective aldol reactions to furnish either syn or anti products. The key role of the solvents in determining the regioselectivity is also demonstrated. The solvent‐dependent reversibility of the α‐addition provides precise control over the regioselectivity, permitting the selective formation of either α‐products or γ‐products. Notably, the regio‐ and diastereoselectivity can be governed by the choice of solvent and the presence of Lewis acids. Our method can be applied to construct the 5‐hydroxy‐1,3‐dicarbonyl framework, which is difficult to obtain from previously reported dienolates. This study thus not only provides new insights into dienolate chemistry but also establishes a versatile platform for the diverse synthesis of structurally distinct molecules.
item.content:encoded:
Achieving selective aldol reactions using dienolates as nucleophiles is challenging due to the potential formation of up to eight different regioisomeric and stereoisomeric aldol adducts. This study demonstrates that Ge dienolates can facilitate highly diastereoselective aldol reactions. Furthermore, the key role of the solvents is also clarified. The solvent-dependent reversibility of the α-addition provides precise control over the regioselectivity. Both regioselectivity and diastereoselectivity are influenced by the choice of solvent and the presence of Lewis acids.


Enolates are essential nucleophiles for the formation of CC bonds. In particular, dienolates, i.e., π-extended enolates that contain a conjugated additional CC bond alongside the enolate moiety, are valuable nucleophiles for the synthesis of π-conjugated hydroxy ketones. Alas, selective aldol reactions of dienolates remain challenging because they can yield up to eight possible regioisomeric (α- or γ-position) and stereoisomeric (syn or anti) aldol adducts. Here, we present the synthesis of Ge dienolates via the (4 + 1) cycloaddition of allenones and GeCl2 together with an investigation of their reactivity. Facilitated by the rigidity of their cyclic structures, the Ge dienolates undergo highly diastereoselective aldol reactions to furnish either syn or anti products. The key role of the solvents in determining the regioselectivity is also demonstrated. The solvent-dependent reversibility of the α-addition provides precise control over the regioselectivity, permitting the selective formation of either α-products or γ-products. Notably, the regio- and diastereoselectivity can be governed by the choice of solvent and the presence of Lewis acids. Our method can be applied to construct the 5-hydroxy-1,3-dicarbonyl framework, which is difficult to obtain from previously reported dienolates. This study thus not only provides new insights into dienolate chemistry but also establishes a versatile platform for the diverse synthesis of structurally distinct molecules.
item.dc:creator: Taishi Nojima, Akihito Konishi, Makoto Yasuda
item.dc:title: Aldol Reactions of Germyl Dienolates With Precise Control Over Regioselectivity and Perfect Diastereoselectivity

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Direct Oxidative Esterification of Alcohols over Oxygen Vacancy Decorated Pd@Bi2O2CO3 Catalyst Supported on N‐Doped Carbon Black
item.dc:description:
A Pd@Bi2O2CO3 catalyst with oxygen vacancies supported on N‐doped carbon black (NCB) was developed based on wet chemistry method. The carrier effect, oxygen vacancies, and synergy between Pd and Bi2O2CO3 ensure an outstanding catalytic activity at a relatively low Pd loading (< 2 wt%), achieving the balance between the catalytic efficiency and cost towards direct oxidative esterification of alcohols to methyl benzoate at a high raw material feeding.
Direct oxidative esterification of alcohols to methyl benzoate has drawn tremendous attention due to the atom economy and environmental benignity. In this work, we developed a Pd@Bi2O2CO3 catalyst with oxygen vacancies supported on N‐doped carbon black (NCB) based on a wet chemistry method. The acidification pretreatment offers NCB with large quantities of oxygen‐containing functional groups for the anchoring of the active component. The oxygen vacancies can help adsorb and activate O2 molecules so as to accelerate the direct oxidative esterification reaction. Most importantly, Bi2O2CO3 plays a key role in modulating the electronic properties of Pd sites, ensuring an enhanced catalytic activity at a relatively low Pd loading (<2 wt%). The Pd@Bi2O2CO3(2/5)/NCB catalyst shows a state‐of‐the‐art activity towards the direct oxidative esterification reaction of benzyl alcohol with methanol to generate methyl benzoate (conversion of >99.9% for benzyl alcohol, selectivity of 95.3% for methyl benzoate), excellent cycling performance, and desirable universality towards various aromatic alcohols under mild conditions. This work presents an environmentally friendly and efficient catalytic system for aromatic alcohol oxidative esterification, demonstrating promising potential for practical industrial application.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd@Bi2O2CO3 catalyst with oxygen vacancies supported on N-doped carbon black (NCB) was developed based on wet chemistry method. The carrier effect, oxygen vacancies, and synergy between Pd and Bi2O2CO3 ensure an outstanding catalytic activity at a relatively low Pd loading (< 2 wt%), achieving the balance between the catalytic efficiency and cost towards direct oxidative esterification of alcohols to methyl benzoate at a high raw material feeding.


Direct oxidative esterification of alcohols to methyl benzoate has drawn tremendous attention due to the atom economy and environmental benignity. In this work, we developed a Pd@Bi2O2CO3 catalyst with oxygen vacancies supported on N-doped carbon black (NCB) based on a wet chemistry method. The acidification pretreatment offers NCB with large quantities of oxygen-containing functional groups for the anchoring of the active component. The oxygen vacancies can help adsorb and activate O2 molecules so as to accelerate the direct oxidative esterification reaction. Most importantly, Bi2O2CO3 plays a key role in modulating the electronic properties of Pd sites, ensuring an enhanced catalytic activity at a relatively low Pd loading (<2 wt%). The Pd@Bi2O2CO3(2/5)/NCB catalyst shows a state-of-the-art activity towards the direct oxidative esterification reaction of benzyl alcohol with methanol to generate methyl benzoate (conversion of >99.9% for benzyl alcohol, selectivity of 95.3% for methyl benzoate), excellent cycling performance, and desirable universality towards various aromatic alcohols under mild conditions. This work presents an environmentally friendly and efficient catalytic system for aromatic alcohol oxidative esterification, demonstrating promising potential for practical industrial application.
item.dc:creator: Ruji Feng, Jiawei Xia, Xingyue Qian, Shilong Zhou, Guangyu He, Haiqun Chen
item.dc:title: Direct Oxidative Esterification of Alcohols over Oxygen Vacancy Decorated Pd@Bi2O2CO3 Catalyst Supported on N‐Doped Carbon Black

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Cyclopropyl‐Boron Compounds as Versatile Synthetic Linchpins: Catalytic, Radical, and Base‐Mediated Approaches
item.dc:description:
This review presents contemporary strategies for constructing cyclopropyl‐boron frameworks and highlights how catalytic, radical, and base‐mediated pathways shape their reactivity. It examines mechanistic features that govern stereocontrol and functional‐group compatibility, emphasizing methods that streamline access to versatile intermediates. The article provides practical guidance for applying these transformations in modern synthetic design.
Cyclopropyl‐boron skeletons have emerged as powerful synthetic intermediates due to their structural compactness, unique reactivity, and broad utility in modern molecular construction. This review highlights the major radical, catalytic, and base‐mediated strategies that enable the selective formation of Boryl‐tethered cyclopropanes from alkenes, alkynes, and cyclopropenes. Key developments across metal carbenoid transfer, hydroborylation, C–H borylation, and intramolecular substitution pathways are critically evaluated through mechanistic and stereochemical insight. Recent advances in asymmetric catalysis, carbonylative cyclopropanation, and photochemical approaches are elaborated for their contributions towards expanding substrate scope and functional diversity. Together, these methodologies define a rapidly growing toolbox that increasingly supports the streamlined synthesis of complex, medicinally relevant cyclopropyl‐boron motifs.
item.content:encoded:
This review presents contemporary strategies for constructing cyclopropyl-boron frameworks and highlights how catalytic, radical, and base-mediated pathways shape their reactivity. It examines mechanistic features that govern stereocontrol and functional-group compatibility, emphasizing methods that streamline access to versatile intermediates. The article provides practical guidance for applying these transformations in modern synthetic design.


Cyclopropyl-boron skeletons have emerged as powerful synthetic intermediates due to their structural compactness, unique reactivity, and broad utility in modern molecular construction. This review highlights the major radical, catalytic, and base-mediated strategies that enable the selective formation of Boryl-tethered cyclopropanes from alkenes, alkynes, and cyclopropenes. Key developments across metal carbenoid transfer, hydroborylation, C–H borylation, and intramolecular substitution pathways are critically evaluated through mechanistic and stereochemical insight. Recent advances in asymmetric catalysis, carbonylative cyclopropanation, and photochemical approaches are elaborated for their contributions towards expanding substrate scope and functional diversity. Together, these methodologies define a rapidly growing toolbox that increasingly supports the streamlined synthesis of complex, medicinally relevant cyclopropyl-boron motifs.
item.dc:creator: Kanak Kanti Das
item.dc:title: Cyclopropyl‐Boron Compounds as Versatile Synthetic Linchpins: Catalytic, Radical, and Base‐Mediated Approaches

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Nickel Nanoparticles Prepared via Colloidal Synthesis as CO2 Hydrogenation Catalysts on Various Support Materials
item.dc:description:
Small aromatic ligands are used to synthesize Ni particles between 1 and 4.4 nm. After deposition on various supports, an alkylating agent and subsequent reduction procedure are used to remove the ligands. On SiO2, the Ni particles remained small, but on Al2O3 they sintered severely. The prepared catalysts were tested in CO2 hydrogenation to methane resulting in varying selectivity and activity.
Conflicting structure‐sensitivity trends were reported for CO2 hydrogenation over Ni‐based catalyst materials from different laboratories. Colloidal synthesis provides not only great control over catalyst properties which could enable a systematic study of structure sensitivity but also challenges related to ligand removal and metal nanoparticle sintering. Here, we used colloidal synthesis to synthesize Ni nanoparticles of different sizes, deposited them on various supports at constant weight loading, and tested them for catalytic CO2 hydrogenation. The usage of small N‐containing ligands, such as mesitylaniline, benzophenone imine, and benzonitrile, yielded Ni nanoparticles with sizes ranging between 1.0 and 4.4 nm. Ni nanoparticles of 2.1 ± 0.3 nm were supported on SiO2, Al2O3, MgAl2O4, CeO2, and TiO2. To remove the ligands, we used an alkylating agent treatment. After a reduction step, the Ni particles remained small on SiO2 but sintered on the other supports, especially on Ni/Al2O3, where Ni particles grew to 20–30 nm. The catalyst materials were tested in CO2 hydrogenation and showed strong variation in activity and selectivity. Overall, this work provides a platform to design and study small Ni metal nanoparticles for structure‐sensitivity studies and highlights the challenges for colloidal particle activation and stabilization on different supports in catalytic CO2 hydrogenation.
item.content:encoded:
Small aromatic ligands are used to synthesize Ni particles between 1 and 4.4 nm. After deposition on various supports, an alkylating agent and subsequent reduction procedure are used to remove the ligands. On SiO2, the Ni particles remained small, but on Al2O3 they sintered severely. The prepared catalysts were tested in CO2 hydrogenation to methane resulting in varying selectivity and activity.


Conflicting structure-sensitivity trends were reported for CO2 hydrogenation over Ni-based catalyst materials from different laboratories. Colloidal synthesis provides not only great control over catalyst properties which could enable a systematic study of structure sensitivity but also challenges related to ligand removal and metal nanoparticle sintering. Here, we used colloidal synthesis to synthesize Ni nanoparticles of different sizes, deposited them on various supports at constant weight loading, and tested them for catalytic CO2 hydrogenation. The usage of small N-containing ligands, such as mesitylaniline, benzophenone imine, and benzonitrile, yielded Ni nanoparticles with sizes ranging between 1.0 and 4.4 nm. Ni nanoparticles of 2.1 ± 0.3 nm were supported on SiO2, Al2O3, MgAl2O4, CeO2, and TiO2. To remove the ligands, we used an alkylating agent treatment. After a reduction step, the Ni particles remained small on SiO2 but sintered on the other supports, especially on Ni/Al2O3, where Ni particles grew to 20–30 nm. The catalyst materials were tested in CO2 hydrogenation and showed strong variation in activity and selectivity. Overall, this work provides a platform to design and study small Ni metal nanoparticles for structure-sensitivity studies and highlights the challenges for colloidal particle activation and stabilization on different supports in catalytic CO2 hydrogenation.
item.dc:creator: Bram T. Kappé, Metin Sen, Esther Groeneveld, Savannah Turner, Ward van der Stam, Matteo Monai, Bert M. Weckhuysen
item.dc:title: Nickel Nanoparticles Prepared via Colloidal Synthesis as CO2 Hydrogenation Catalysts on Various Support Materials

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Interfacial Modulation of Composite Structures for Activating Ag3PO4 Toward Efficient and Stable Electrocatalytic Ethylene Epoxidation
item.dc:description:
This study constructs a graphene oxide‐wrapped Ag3PO4 composite electrocatalyst, which significantly enhances the performance of ethylene electro‐epoxidation. The catalyst achieves an ethylene oxide production rate of 98 μmol·cm−2·h−1 with a Faradaic efficiency of 35% at 1.85 V vs. RHE and demonstrates excellent stability for over 10 h, effectively overcoming the inherent poor conductivity and structural instability of Ag3PO4.
The development of efficient and stable electrocatalysts represents a pivotal challenge in realizing a green process for the direct electrocatalytic epoxidation of ethylene. This study addresses the limitations of silver phosphate (Ag3PO4), a material with promising catalytic activity that is hindered by intrinsically low electrical conductivity and structural instability. Through an interface engineering strategy, we design and synthesize a composite catalyst of Ag3PO4 encapsulated by graphene oxide (GO), denoted as Ag3PO4@GO. In this composite structure, GO not only acts as a conductive scaffold that significantly enhances charge transfer efficiency but also effectively suppresses the agglomeration of Ag3PO4 nanoparticles via an interfacial anchoring effect. Systematic characterization confirms a significant electronic interaction between GO and Ag3PO4, which optimizes the electronic structure of the active sites. Electrocatalytic performance tests demonstrate that the Ag3PO4@GO catalyst achieves an ethylene oxide production rate of 98 μmol·cm−2·h−1 with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 35% at 1.85 V (vs. RHE), along with excellent operational stability for over 10 h. This work, by designing a multifunctional support, simultaneously mitigates the inherent conductivity and stability issues of Ag3PO4 catalysts, thereby offering a new avenue for developing high‐performance electrocatalysts for olefin epoxidation.
item.content:encoded:
This study constructs a graphene oxide-wrapped Ag3PO4 composite electrocatalyst, which significantly enhances the performance of ethylene electro-epoxidation. The catalyst achieves an ethylene oxide production rate of 98 μmol·cm−2·h−1 with a Faradaic efficiency of 35% at 1.85 V vs. RHE and demonstrates excellent stability for over 10 h, effectively overcoming the inherent poor conductivity and structural instability of Ag3PO4.


The development of efficient and stable electrocatalysts represents a pivotal challenge in realizing a green process for the direct electrocatalytic epoxidation of ethylene. This study addresses the limitations of silver phosphate (Ag3PO4), a material with promising catalytic activity that is hindered by intrinsically low electrical conductivity and structural instability. Through an interface engineering strategy, we design and synthesize a composite catalyst of Ag3PO4 encapsulated by graphene oxide (GO), denoted as Ag3PO4@GO. In this composite structure, GO not only acts as a conductive scaffold that significantly enhances charge transfer efficiency but also effectively suppresses the agglomeration of Ag3PO4 nanoparticles via an interfacial anchoring effect. Systematic characterization confirms a significant electronic interaction between GO and Ag3PO4, which optimizes the electronic structure of the active sites. Electrocatalytic performance tests demonstrate that the Ag3PO4@GO catalyst achieves an ethylene oxide production rate of 98 μmol·cm−2·h−1 with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 35% at 1.85 V (vs. RHE), along with excellent operational stability for over 10 h. This work, by designing a multifunctional support, simultaneously mitigates the inherent conductivity and stability issues of Ag3PO4 catalysts, thereby offering a new avenue for developing high-performance electrocatalysts for olefin epoxidation.
item.dc:creator: Yinghua Zhang, Xifeng Yu, Hui Li, Sijia Liu, Siwen Yang, Hongliang Liu, Yan Lin, Zhongtao Li
item.dc:title: Interfacial Modulation of Composite Structures for Activating Ag3PO4 Toward Efficient and Stable Electrocatalytic Ethylene Epoxidation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper(I)‐Photocatalyzed Meta‐Selective Sulfonylation of Pyridines With Sulfonyl Chlorides
item.dc:description:
A copper(I)‐photocatalyzed, oxidant‐ and precious‐metal‐free method is developed for meta‐selective sulfonylation of pyridines with sulfonyl chlorides.
A copper(I)‐photocatalyzed protocol for the meta‐selective sulfonylation of pyridines with sulfonyl chlorides is described. The operationally simple reaction proceeds under precious‐metal‐ and external‐oxidant‐free conditions and exhibits high regioselectivity, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional‐group tolerance. The mild protocol enables late‐stage conversion of pharmaceutically relevant molecules into the desired sulfonated pyridines in acceptable yields, offering an efficient and sustainable route for drug diversification and structural modification.
item.content:encoded:
A copper(I)-photocatalyzed, oxidant- and precious-metal-free method is developed for meta-selective sulfonylation of pyridines with sulfonyl chlorides.


A copper(I)-photocatalyzed protocol for the meta-selective sulfonylation of pyridines with sulfonyl chlorides is described. The operationally simple reaction proceeds under precious-metal- and external-oxidant-free conditions and exhibits high regioselectivity, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional-group tolerance. The mild protocol enables late-stage conversion of pharmaceutically relevant molecules into the desired sulfonated pyridines in acceptable yields, offering an efficient and sustainable route for drug diversification and structural modification.
item.dc:creator: Qian Zhang, Kai Sun, Panjie Xiang, Xu Li, Qiyan Lv, Jinquan Wang, Xiaolan Chen, Lingbo Qu, Bing Yu
item.dc:title: Copper(I)‐Photocatalyzed Meta‐Selective Sulfonylation of Pyridines With Sulfonyl Chlorides

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Promoted Iron‐Catalyzed Atom Transfer Radical Addition for the Chlorosulfonylation of Alkenes
item.dc:description:
Iron‐based photocatalysis remains relatively underexplored due to its labile nature and short excited‐state lifetimes. We present a protocol for atom transfer radical addition reactions using a ligand‐free iron catalysis, [n‐Bu4N][Fe(Cl)4], enabling the one‐step chlorosulfonylation of alkenes. The approach provides an alternative to Ru, Ir, and copper photocatalysis for the titled transformation.
Herein, we present an iron‐catalyzed atom transfer radical addition (ATRA) reaction. The approach exhibits low catalysis loading, broad substrate scope, and high atom economy through the use inexpensive and readily available reagents. Notably, this operationally simple strategy provides an alternative to conventional Ru, Ir, and Cu‐photocatalyzed ATRA reactions, and mechanistic studies via intermediate interceptions elucidate the critical role played by the catalysis in directing the transformation. The robustness and synthetic utility were further demonstrated by the late‐stage functionalizations and gram‐scale reactions, which highlighting its value for industrial applications.
item.content:encoded:
Iron-based photocatalysis remains relatively underexplored due to its labile nature and short excited-state lifetimes. We present a protocol for atom transfer radical addition reactions using a ligand-free iron catalysis, [n-Bu4N][Fe(Cl)4], enabling the one-step chlorosulfonylation of alkenes. The approach provides an alternative to Ru, Ir, and copper photocatalysis for the titled transformation.


Herein, we present an iron-catalyzed atom transfer radical addition (ATRA) reaction. The approach exhibits low catalysis loading, broad substrate scope, and high atom economy through the use inexpensive and readily available reagents. Notably, this operationally simple strategy provides an alternative to conventional Ru, Ir, and Cu-photocatalyzed ATRA reactions, and mechanistic studies via intermediate interceptions elucidate the critical role played by the catalysis in directing the transformation. The robustness and synthetic utility were further demonstrated by the late-stage functionalizations and gram-scale reactions, which highlighting its value for industrial applications.
item.dc:creator: Jianan Li, Yaqian Wu, Jiaqi Niu, Feng Zhao
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Promoted Iron‐Catalyzed Atom Transfer Radical Addition for the Chlorosulfonylation of Alkenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Difluorocarbene‐Mediated C(sp3)–N Bond Cleavage and In Situ Formylation of Tertiary Amines
item.dc:description:
A sustainable mechanochemical method for C(sp3)–N bond cleavage and in situ N‐formylation of tertiary amines is developed using a difluorocarbene precursor. The transformation proceeds under mild ball‐milling conditions, demonstrating a broad substrate scope, excellent functional group tolerance, and efficient conversions of diverse aliphatic and heterocyclic amines into N‐formamides. The strategy is validated through a gram‐scale synthesis, molecular derivatizations, and preparations of macrocyclic molecules, highlighting its potential for applications in natural product synthesis and drug development.
C(sp3)–N bond cleavage is a pivotal transformation in organic synthesis. To the best of our knowledge, mechanochemical methods are unknown. Herein, we present a sustainable mechanochemical strategy in which a difluorocarbene precursor effects direct cleavage of tertiary amine C(sp3)–N bonds with concurrent in situ N‐formylation. The approach overcomes limitations of conventional methods, exhibiting an excellent substrate scope and a broad functional group tolerance. Diverse aliphatic and heterocyclic amines are efficiently converted into N‐formamides under mild ball‐milling conditions. The versatility of the method is validated by gram‐scale syntheses, subsequent molecular derivatizations, and syntheses of macrocyclic molecules. In addition, mechanochemical difluoromethylative ring‐openings of thioethers and ethers are described, highlighting potential applications in natural product syntheses and drug development.
item.content:encoded:
A sustainable mechanochemical method for C(sp3)–N bond cleavage and in situ N-formylation of tertiary amines is developed using a difluorocarbene precursor. The transformation proceeds under mild ball-milling conditions, demonstrating a broad substrate scope, excellent functional group tolerance, and efficient conversions of diverse aliphatic and heterocyclic amines into N-formamides. The strategy is validated through a gram-scale synthesis, molecular derivatizations, and preparations of macrocyclic molecules, highlighting its potential for applications in natural product synthesis and drug development.


C(sp3)–N bond cleavage is a pivotal transformation in organic synthesis. To the best of our knowledge, mechanochemical methods are unknown. Herein, we present a sustainable mechanochemical strategy in which a difluorocarbene precursor effects direct cleavage of tertiary amine C(sp3)–N bonds with concurrent in situ N-formylation. The approach overcomes limitations of conventional methods, exhibiting an excellent substrate scope and a broad functional group tolerance. Diverse aliphatic and heterocyclic amines are efficiently converted into N-formamides under mild ball-milling conditions. The versatility of the method is validated by gram-scale syntheses, subsequent molecular derivatizations, and syntheses of macrocyclic molecules. In addition, mechanochemical difluoromethylative ring-openings of thioethers and ethers are described, highlighting potential applications in natural product syntheses and drug development.
item.dc:creator: Yijie Hu, Peng Wu, Chandan Chittapriya Sahu, Kari Rissanen, Carsten Bolm
item.dc:title: Mechanochemical Difluorocarbene‐Mediated C(sp3)–N Bond Cleavage and In Situ Formylation of Tertiary Amines

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemo‐ and Enantioselective Nickel‐Catalyzed Alkenylative Coupling of Alkynes and Aldehydes or Ketones for the Facile Synthesis of 1,3‐Dienols
item.dc:description:
A nickel‐catalyzed alkenylative coupling of aldehydes(ketones), alkynes, and alkenyl boronic acids is reported, providing a modular and efficient route to stereodefined 1,3‐dienols from readily available substrates. Meanwhile, an enantioselective variant enables the synthesis of chiral dienols with high enantiocontrol (up to 99% ee), and an intramolecular extension allows efficient access to chiral tertiary alcohols bearing exocyclic dienes.
A nickel‐catalyzed alkenylative coupling of aldehydes(ketones), alkynes, and alkenyl boronic acids is reported, providing a modular and efficient route to stereodefined 1,3‐dienols from readily available substrates. This transformation exploits the controlled reactivity of oxanickelacycle intermediates and avoids the use of nucleophilic or moisture‐sensitive dienyl organometallic reagents. Broad substrate scope is observed for all three components, delivering multisubstituted dienols with high regioselectivity and good to excellent yields. An enantioselective variant employing a P‐chiral monophosphine ligand furnishes chiral dienols with good enantiocontrol (up to 99% ee). In addition, an intramolecular version with 1,6/1,7‐ynones enables the asymmetric synthesis of tertiary alcohols bearing exocyclic dienes. This work significantly expands the synthetic toolkit for dienol synthesis and showcases the versatile reactivity of oxanickelacycle intermediates in multicomponent coupling.
item.content:encoded:
A nickel-catalyzed alkenylative coupling of aldehydes(ketones), alkynes, and alkenyl boronic acids is reported, providing a modular and efficient route to stereodefined 1,3-dienols from readily available substrates. Meanwhile, an enantioselective variant enables the synthesis of chiral dienols with high enantiocontrol (up to 99% ee), and an intramolecular extension allows efficient access to chiral tertiary alcohols bearing exocyclic dienes.


A nickel-catalyzed alkenylative coupling of aldehydes(ketones), alkynes, and alkenyl boronic acids is reported, providing a modular and efficient route to stereodefined 1,3-dienols from readily available substrates. This transformation exploits the controlled reactivity of oxanickelacycle intermediates and avoids the use of nucleophilic or moisture-sensitive dienyl organometallic reagents. Broad substrate scope is observed for all three components, delivering multisubstituted dienols with high regioselectivity and good to excellent yields. An enantioselective variant employing a P-chiral monophosphine ligand furnishes chiral dienols with good enantiocontrol (up to 99% ee). In addition, an intramolecular version with 1,6/1,7-ynones enables the asymmetric synthesis of tertiary alcohols bearing exocyclic dienes. This work significantly expands the synthetic toolkit for dienol synthesis and showcases the versatile reactivity of oxanickelacycle intermediates in multicomponent coupling.
item.dc:creator: Zhe‐Xuan Li, Si‐Chen Tao, Mengcan Xia, Hong‐Bin Ma, Yan‐Long Zheng
item.dc:title: Chemo‐ and Enantioselective Nickel‐Catalyzed Alkenylative Coupling of Alkynes and Aldehydes or Ketones for the Facile Synthesis of 1,3‐Dienols
